questions.question: Prove that for any positive integer $n$, the expression $n^3 - n$ is always divisible by $6$. Using this result, or otherwise, show that the product of three consecutive positive integers is always divisible by $6$.
questions.answer: The expression $n^3 - n$ can be factored as $(n-1)n(n+1)$, which represents the product of three consecutive integers. Since out of any three consecutive integers, at least one must be a multiple of $2$ and exactly one must be a multiple of $3$, their product must be divisible by $2 \times 3 = 6$.

steps: Factorize the expression $n^3 - n$ to get $n(n^2 - 1) = (n-1)n(n+1)$.
Identify that $(n-1)n(n+1)$ represents the product of three consecutive integers.
Explain that in any set of three consecutive integers, at least one must be even (divisible by $2$).
Explain that in any set of three consecutive integers, exactly one must be a multiple of $3$.
Conclude that since the product is divisible by both $2$ and $3$, and $\text{gcd}(2, 3) = 1$, the product must be divisible by their product, $6$.
solution.derivation: $n^3 - n = n(n^2 - 1) \\ = (n-1)n(n+1) \\ \text{Let } k = n-1, \text{ then the product is } k(k+1)(k+2) \\ \text{Since in any three consecutive integers, at least one is divisible by } 2, \text{ let } 2 | P \\ \text{Since in any three consecutive integers, one is divisible by } 3, \text{ let } 3 | P \\ \text{Since } \text{gcd}(2, 3) = 1, \text{ it follows that } 2 \times 3 | P \\ \therefore 6 | (n-1)n(n+1) \\ \therefore 6 | (n^3 - n)$
